title: Rowland's concave grating: the mirror that ruled the solar spectrum
intro: Henry Augustus Rowland was Johns Hopkins University's first professor of physics, hired in 1875 at age twenty-seven on the strength of a paper Maxwell had personally forwarded to the Annalen der Physik after American journals had refused it. By the early 1880s he was bored with everything except machines. The instrument he wanted next was a ruling engine that could cut diffraction-grating lines straighter and finer than any in existence. The grating he ended up producing, ruled almost as an afterthought on a polished concave mirror rather than a flat one, dispensed with the two expensive achromatic lenses every spectrograph of the day needed and pushed the resolving power R = λ/Δλ (the smallest fractional wavelength difference an instrument can split) past 80,000. For the next sixty years the international wavelength standard, the Fraunhofer atlas of the solar spectrum, and the working spectrographs in nearly every observatory on Earth ran on Rowland's gratings.

blocks.text: Joseph von Fraunhofer in Munich ruled the first practical diffraction gratings around 1821, fine gold wires stretched across a brass frame at densities of 200 to 300 lines per millimeter. With them he mapped 574 dark lines (the absorption signatures of cooler atoms in the Sun's outer atmosphere, the chromosphere) across the solar spectrum and labelled the strongest with letters A through K, a labelling that still anchors the wavelength scale a century later. Fraunhofer died of tuberculosis in 1826, age thirty-nine. The wire-grid technique stagnated for the next forty years.

The American Lewis Morris Rutherfurd, a New York lawyer who had abandoned his law practice to run a private observatory on West 11th Street, took up the problem in the 1860s by ruling gratings with a diamond stylus on speculum metal, a tin-bronze alloy reflective enough at sixty-five percent to work as a mirror. By 1869 his best gratings carried 17,000 ruled lines per inch over half-inch widths, giving a total ruled-line count N of about 8,500 and a resolving power R = mN of about 17,000 in second order, where m is the integer diffraction order. The gratings were the world's best, and every spectroscopist who could afford one wanted one. But every flat reflection grating, like every flat transmission grating before it, demanded two large achromatic lenses to be useful at all: a collimating lens (collimator: an element that turns diverging light from a point source into a parallel beam) before the grating to feed it parallel rays from the entrance slit, and a camera lens (camera: an element that brings parallel rays from a single direction to a sharp point on a photographic plate) after to focus each wavelength's diffracted parallel beam back to a sharp line image.

The lenses were the bottleneck. Achromatic doublets (lenses made of two glass elements chosen so their dispersions cancel at two specific wavelengths) of the necessary aperture cost more than the grating they served, suffered residual chromatic aberration across the visible band, and went badly out of focus in the ultraviolet where the most diagnostically important atomic lines sat. Rowland began in 1881 building a ruling engine of his own in the basement of the physics building at Hopkins. By the end of 1882 the engine could rule a grating densities approaching 15,000 lines per inch across a fourteen-centimeter blank, putting the total line count N near 80,000, an order of magnitude beyond Rutherfurd's. Then in early 1883 he tried the move nobody had attempted before: he ruled his next blank not on a polished flat but on a polished concave spherical mirror, and the lenses fell out of the optical train altogether.

sections.heading: 2. The Rowland circle: dispersing and focusing in one element

blocks.text: A grating ruled on a flat substrate, illuminated by a parallel beam, diffracts into discrete orders at angles fixed by d sin θ_m = mλ, where d is the line spacing (the distance between adjacent ruled lines). The flat geometry asks for parallel light in and parallel light out, hence the two lenses. Rule the same line pattern on a polished concave spherical mirror of radius R, and the curvature contributes a second optical fact alongside the diffraction. The surface acts as a focusing mirror, the way any concave mirror focuses, so the diffracted orders come to sharp wavelength images at finite distance from the grating with no separate camera lens.

Rowland worked out the controlling geometry in 1883. Draw a circle of diameter R tangent to the concave grating at the grating's center. This is the Rowland circle. Place the entrance slit S anywhere on the circle, at some angle α from the grating normal (the line perpendicular to the grating surface at its center). The grating G itself sits on the circle by construction. The geometric statement is that every diffraction order m, evaluated at every wavelength λ, brings its image to focus at another point F on the same Rowland circle, located at the angle β from the grating normal given by the concave-grating equation:

blocks.caption: d is the line spacing of the ruled grating, the distance between adjacent ruled lines. α is the angle of incidence at the grating, measured from the surface normal, fixed once the slit is placed on the Rowland circle. β is the diffraction angle of the spectral line of interest, measured from the same normal at the point where the wavelength comes to focus. m is the integer diffraction order (m = 1, 2, 3, ...). λ is the wavelength being imaged. The flat-grating equation d sin θ = mλ is the special case α = 0, β = θ. The added sin α term simply allows oblique illumination, which on the Rowland circle is the natural mounting since the slit is placed off the grating's normal.

blocks.text: The geometric miracle is that there are no lens errors because there are no lenses. The grating's focal length and dispersion are both set by the same curvature R, locked together by the Rowland-circle geometry. The wavelength range across which the spectrograph stays in focus is in principle unlimited, since the focusing is geometric rather than refractive: a single Rowland-circle mounting could cover from the deep ultraviolet (where glass lenses absorbed and went chromatic) clear through to the near infrared on a single photographic plate. That was the practical revolution. The whole optical train collapsed from grating-plus-collimator-plus-camera to grating-only.

blocks.caption: The Rowland circle (dashed) is the locus on which the slit S, the concave grating G, and the entire focused spectrum simultaneously sit. The grating is fixed at the bottom of the circle; its normal points vertically up the dashed gray line. Drag the α slider to place the slit on the circle at incidence angle α from the normal; the colored dots are the focused images of monochromatic wavelengths from 400 to 700 nm, each computed from d(sin α + sin β) = mλ with grating spacing d = 1.5 μm. Notice there is no lens anywhere; the curvature of the grating handles all the focusing. Toggle between m = 1 and m = 2 to see the same wavelengths land at different angles in different orders, and notice how the second-order spectrum spreads over a larger arc than the first.

quiz.question: A Rowland-mounted concave grating has radius of curvature R = 6.5 m (a typical Rowland-era choice) and line spacing d = 1.76 μm (corresponding to 14,438 lines per inch). With the entrance slit placed at normal incidence (α = 0) on the Rowland circle, what diffraction angle β does the sodium D line at λ = 589 nm form in first order m = 1, and what role does the curvature R play in setting β?
options: β = arcsin(2λ/d) ≈ 41.9°. The curvature R enters the grating equation as a multiplicative factor and shifts the diffraction angle upward from the flat-grating value at the same d.

β = arcsin(λ/d) ≈ 19.5°. The curvature R does not appear in the grating equation at all; it only sets where on the Rowland circle the focused image of that wavelength forms (the linear plate scale, in millimeters per nanometer). The diffraction geometry at the grating is identical to that of a flat grating with the same d at the same incidence angle.
β = 0°, because at normal incidence (α = 0) the m = 1 diffracted ray retraces the incident ray back to the slit, the same retracing that defines specular reflection.
β cannot be computed from the information given; the Rowland-circle geometry requires knowing the diffraction-efficiency curve of the grating before β can be specified.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 writes the equation wrong by inserting m = 2 instead of m = 1 and bolts on a phantom curvature dependence; the curvature shifts where the spectrum focuses but not the angle at which it leaves the grating. Option 2 conflates m = 0 (the specular zero-order reflection, which does retrace at normal incidence) with m = 1 (the first-order diffraction, which does not). Option 3 is wrong: diffraction efficiency sets how much energy each order receives, not at which angle the order forms. The honest answer is option 1. The concave-grating equation is d(sin α + sin β) = mλ. At normal incidence (α = 0) it reduces to d sin β = mλ, identical in form to the flat-grating equation. For λ = 589 nm, d = 1760 nm, m = 1: sin β = 0.335, β ≈ 19.5°. The radius R does not appear in this equation at all. R sets the diameter of the Rowland circle and therefore the linear separation between focused spectral lines on the photographic plate (the plate scale, in mm per Angstrom), but it does not change the angle at which a given wavelength leaves the grating in a given order. The curvature's job is focusing, not dispersing, and the two roles are cleanly separable. This separability is exactly the structural elegance of the concave-grating mounting that put Rowland-style spectrographs in nearly every major observatory by 1900.

sections.heading: 3. The ruling engine: lapping a lead screw to twenty nanometers

blocks.text: The Rowland circle was a geometric insight in principle accessible to anyone who could draw a tangent. The grating that made it work, with N approaching 100,000 ruled lines, with line-to-line positions accurate to a few parts in ten million, with smooth amplitude variation across the ruled width, was a mechanical achievement of an entirely different order. The 1882 Rowland ruling engine was the artifact that mattered. Its heart was a precision lead screw of Rowland's own design, lapped over months in the Hopkins shop until the residual cyclic deviation of the thread fell below tens of nanometers cumulative across the screw's working length.

Periodic errors in the screw revealed themselves on every test ruling as 'ghosts': false spectral lines flanking each real line, spaced at the screw's pitch frequency in wavelength, the unmistakable signature of any cyclic ruling error. Rowland and his machinists ran test rulings, photographed the spectra, measured the ghost intensities, and went back to the lapping bench. The engine room itself was held to a tenth of a degree Celsius by water circulation through the cast-iron base, because a one-degree drift would expand or contract the brass enough to ruin a fourteen-centimeter ruling. Each line was cut by a single diamond tool; the blank advanced by the screw between cuts, the diamond pressure held constant by a counterweight. A single fourteen-centimeter grating took twenty to thirty hours of continuous ruling, and the engine ran day and night for months at a stretch.

Rowland did not sell the gratings. He distributed them at cost (typically twenty to fifty dollars apiece, perhaps a thousand in present-day dollars) to any researcher whose intended use he approved. By the mid-1890s the Hopkins engine had produced several hundred Rowland gratings, and most of the major spectroscopic discoveries of that decade had been made on them: the resolution of the sodium D doublet into its 0.6 nm fine-structure components (the same doublet Michelson had used to calibrate the standard meter), the identification of the Balmer hydrogen series in the spectra of A-type stars, the first clean ultraviolet line lists for cadmium and zinc that would later anchor every laboratory's wavelength standards. The Rowland grating was at the time the most precisely engineered object in the world, and every other spectroscopist's measurements were limited by Rowland's mechanical tolerances rather than their own.

sections.heading: 4. Resolving power and the free spectral range

blocks.text: The dispersing power Rowland delivered, set against the lens-bottlenecked spectrographs of 1880, is a stack of three numbers. The first is the resolving power R, the dimensionless ratio of operating wavelength λ to the smallest wavelength difference Δλ the instrument can split into two distinct lines at that wavelength. For a grating, R takes a famously simple form derivable in one line from the Rayleigh criterion (the convention that two equal-intensity spectral lines are just resolved when the central maximum of one falls on the first zero of the other) applied to the grating equation:

blocks.caption: R is the spectroscopic resolving power, the operating wavelength λ divided by the smallest wavelength difference Δλ at which the instrument can separate two equal-intensity lines under the Rayleigh criterion. λ is the operating wavelength at which the measurement is being made. Δλ is the minimum resolvable wavelength separation at that λ. m is the integer diffraction order in use. N is the total number of ruled lines actually illuminated by the entrance beam, almost always the full ruled width of the grating. The formula is grating-only; no lens, no slit, no other optical element contributes to it at the diffraction limit. With Rowland's N ≈ 80,000 and m = 2 he routinely worked at R ≈ 160,000, four to five times every flat grating then available.

blocks.text: The second number is the free spectral range, the wavelength interval the instrument can cover in a single order m without the next order overlapping back into the field of view. The free spectral range works out to FSR = λ/m: in first order at λ = 589 nm (sodium D), the FSR is 589 nm, comfortably the whole visible. In second order at 589 nm the FSR is 295 nm: still adequate, but the third-order spectrum at 393 nm (calcium H and K) starts to overlap the second-order red end near 786 nm. Higher orders trade free spectral range for resolving power, and Rowland-class instruments routinely used order-sorting filters or cross-dispersers (a second low-resolution element that separates the orders along an axis perpendicular to the high-resolution dispersion) to keep the orders cleanly separated on the plate.

The third number is the dispersion in millimeters per Angstrom on the photographic plate, set by the Rowland-circle radius R and the order m. Rowland's later mountings used radii of three to six meters, putting the plate scale in second order at roughly five to ten millimeters per Angstrom, large enough that a single twenty-centimeter plate covered only a few hundred Angstroms but resolved every line in that band. The trade-off between spectral coverage per plate and resolution per line is set by geometry, not by lens limitations, and it can be tuned simply by choosing a grating of different curvature for a different job.

quiz.question: A Rowland-class grating has N = 100,000 ruled lines across its full width and is used in second order, m = 2. What resolving power R does it deliver at the sodium D₁ line, λ = 589.6 nm, and what is the minimum wavelength separation Δλ it can split? Now suppose someone proposes doubling the line density to 200,000 lines at the same fourteen-centimeter ruled width (so the line spacing d halves while N doubles). What changes?
options: R = 100,000 and Δλ = 5.9 pm. Doubling the line count to 200,000 doubles N but halves d, leaving R unchanged because the resolving power is R = mN/d and the two effects cancel.

R = 200,000 and Δλ ≈ 2.9 pm. Doubling the ruled line count doubles N, which doubles R to 400,000 and halves Δλ to about 1.5 pm. Doubling the line density at fixed ruled width is the only ruling change that improves R, because R = mN depends only on the integer order m and the illuminated line count N. The line spacing d sets only the diffraction angle β, not the resolving power.

R = 50,000 and Δλ = 11.8 pm. Working in second order halves the effective ruled count because alternate orders divert energy elsewhere, so doubling the ruled count merely restores the second-order R back to 100,000.
R = 200,000 only in the limit of monochromatic input; in practice the finite width of the entrance slit limits R, and doubling N cannot improve R unless the slit is narrowed in step with it.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 introduces a phantom d-dependence in the resolving-power formula. The grating equation contains d, but the resolving-power formula R = mN does not. Halving d at fixed ruled width only changes the diffraction angles β at which the orders form, not the resolving power. Option 2 is wrong about both the order-counting (second order doubles R relative to first order, it does not halve it) and the effect of doubling the ruled count. Option 3 is partially correct in spirit (the finite slit width does set the practical R below the diffraction limit) but wrong as written: the question is asking for the diffraction-limited R, and R = mN is precisely that. The honest answer is option 1. At λ = 589.6 nm with m = 2 and N = 100,000, R = mN = 200,000. The minimum resolvable wavelength difference is Δλ = λ/R = 589.6 nm / 200,000 ≈ 2.9 pm (picometers), or 0.029 Angstroms, comfortably enough to resolve the sodium D doublet (whose D₁ and D₂ components are separated by 0.6 nm = 600 pm). Doubling the ruled count to 200,000 at fixed ruled width doubles R to 400,000 and halves Δλ to roughly 1.5 pm. The line spacing d halves at the same time, which shifts every diffraction angle β to a new value via the grating equation, but the resolving power formula does not depend on d at all. This separation is exactly why Rowland's competition with Rutherfurd ran on ruled line count rather than on cleverness of the grating equation.

sections.heading: 5. 1893 to 1897: the Rowland tables and the helium identification

blocks.text: The instrument Rowland built was a means to an end. The end was a tabulation, accurate enough to serve as the international wavelength standard, of every Fraunhofer line in the solar spectrum. He began photographing the Sun through a Rowland grating in 1888, using a six-meter Rowland-circle mounting bolted to the wall of the physics building at Hopkins, with a fixed slit aligned along the celestial equator and a heliostat (a clock-driven mirror that tracks the Sun across the sky) feeding the slit. The plates covered fifty Angstroms each at a dispersion of about ten millimeters per Angstrom in second order, on photographic plates twenty centimeters long. The slow exposures (typically twenty seconds at the cleanest visible wavelengths, several minutes in the harder ultraviolet) were followed by months of plate measurement at micrometer-driven microscopes, with line positions read to the nearest micron and converted to wavelength through a single calibration constant per plate.

The Preliminary Table of Solar Spectrum Wavelengths appeared in installments in the Astrophysical Journal between 1893 and 1897. It listed roughly 14,000 lines from 297 to 734 nm, each tied to a wavelength accurate (after later vacuum and dispersion corrections) to about a thousandth of an Angstrom, or one part in five million in the absolute wavelength. The International Solar Union adopted Rowland's table as the official wavelength scale at its 1907 meeting in Meudon, six years after his death from diabetes complications at age fifty-two. The next major revision was Charlotte Moore's Revised Multiplet Table at the U.S. Bureau of Standards in 1945, almost half a century later, by which time atomic emission lines from laboratory discharge tubes had finally caught up to Rowland's solar precision.

Read in retrospect, the Rowland table contained the wavelengths other astronomers had to puzzle out by physical identification one line at a time. The yellow line at 587.49 nm, prominent in the chromospheric spectrum captured during the 1868 solar eclipse by Norman Lockyer at Vichy and Pierre Janssen in Guntur (the two men proposed the new element 'helium' from helios, the Greek for Sun), sat in the Rowland table for nearly thirty years without a terrestrial match. There was no terrestrial helium discharge line to compare against until William Ramsay in 1895 boiled the gas out of cleveite (a uranium-bearing mineral that traps helium produced by alpha decay) and ran it through a discharge tube in his University College London laboratory. The cross-identification with the Rowland solar line was immediate: same wavelength, to within Rowland's micron of plate position. The 587.49 nm line became D₃, the third member of the visible sodium D group. The same crossover happened in succession for nebulium (a green line in nebular spectra at 500.7 nm, identified in 1928 by Ira Bowen as the forbidden 1S to 1D transition of doubly-ionized oxygen, O III) and for coronium (a green coronal line at 530.3 nm, identified in 1939 by Walter Grotrian and Bengt Edlén as the forbidden transition of thirteen-times-ionized iron, Fe XIV). Each identification was the final beat in a story whose first measurement had been Rowland's, made with a grating whose line positions held still long enough to wait.

finalQuiz.question: Why was a grating ruled on a concave mirror a larger contribution to spectroscopy than a flat grating of the same ruled line count N would have been, even though the resolving power R = mN depends only on the line count and the order, and not on the substrate curvature? Lay out the geometric, mechanical, and historical strands together.
options: The concave grating could be ruled at higher N because the curved substrate accommodated more ruled lines per unit area than a flat one, so its R was always larger than a flat grating of comparable physical size.

The concave grating offered no fundamental optical advantage; the historical importance came entirely from the precision of Rowland's ruling engine, which would have been equally valuable on flat substrates and was only marketed alongside the concave geometry for branding reasons.

The concave grating eliminated the achromatic-doublet collimator and camera, replacing two expensive chromatic lens elements with one geometric self-focusing optical surface. Geometrically: dispersing and focusing collapsed into a single optical surface, with the slit, the grating, and every focused spectral line all sitting on the Rowland circle. The same R achieved on a flat grating would still need a lens train that imposed chromatic and field-curvature limits at the ultraviolet end (where the most diagnostically important atomic lines sat) and shifted focus across wavelength. Mechanically: Rowland's lapped lead screw delivered N ≈ 80,000 lines per blank with cyclic errors below the picometer regime, an unprecedented combination of count and uniformity. Historically: the Hopkins engine distributed several hundred Rowland gratings worldwide by 1900, and every major spectroscopic discovery of the 1890s (the sodium D doublet's fine-structure separation, the Balmer series in stellar atmospheres, the 14,000-line Rowland tables of solar wavelengths to a thousandth of an Angstrom) was made on Rowland-ruled blanks. The 1895 helium identification by Ramsay from cleveite was a direct readback of the solar D₃ line Rowland had tabulated a decade earlier.

Concave gratings actually had worse R than flat gratings of the same N because the diffracted orders did not stay in geometric focus across the full Rowland circle, so the effective N (the line count that contributed coherently) was always smaller than the ruled N.
finalQuiz.explanation: Option 0 misstates the manufacturing fact: lines per inch is set by the ruling engine, not by the substrate curvature, and Rowland's gratings achieved their high N because his screw was better than anyone else's, not because his blanks were curved. Option 1 misses the optical content: the concave geometry collapsed the entire collimator-plus-camera lens train into one element, an enormous fact that no engine improvement on a flat substrate could have delivered. Option 3 inverts the geometric fact: the Rowland-circle theorem is exactly that the spectrum does stay in focus across the entire circle, by virtue of the grating's curvature. The honest answer is option 2. The combination of three contributions, all from a single instrument, made Rowland's contribution larger than any one strand alone. Geometrically: the same curvature that disperses also focuses, so dispersing and focusing occupied one element rather than three (collimator, grating, camera). The Rowland-circle geometry made the entire spectrum sharp on a single curved focal surface, accessible in the deep ultraviolet where lenses were absorbing and useless. Mechanically: the lapped lead screw delivered cyclic accuracy below the picometer regime and ruled-line counts an order of magnitude beyond Rutherfurd, on the same substrate the optical geometry asked for. Historically: by distributing several hundred gratings at cost rather than selling them, Rowland put the world's best spectroscopic dispersion in the hands of every observatory that asked, and the discoveries of the 1890s (the resolved sodium D doublet, the Balmer series in stellar A-type atmospheres, the Rowland tables themselves) all rode on his blanks. The 1895 cleveite helium identification by Ramsay was the cleanest demonstration of what the Rowland tables made possible: a wavelength tabulated to a thousandth of an Angstrom in solar light in 1893 was matched by a discharge-tube line two years later, settling the identification of the first element discovered outside the Earth.
